statement: 4 (Polynomial Freiman-Ruzsa conjecture) . For any K ⩾ 1 we have the inequality G(K) ≪ K O(1). In other words, any non-empty set A ⊆ FI 2 is contained in the union of O(K O(1)) cosets of a subspace of cardinality at most |A|. FREIMAN IN FINITE FIELDS 3 It follows quickly from [10, Corollary 1.5] and Ruzsa’s cove ring lemma (see e.g. [15, Lemma 2.14]) that G(K) ≪ K O( √ K). This is the best upper bound currently known for G(K). The PFR conjecture allows one to pass from the “combinatoria l information” |A + A| ⩽ K|A| to the “algebraic information” that A is contained in a union of cosets and back again with only polynomial losses in the constants. By c ontrast the use of results such as Theorem 1.3 entail the loss of an exponential, and thi s is necessary in view of Lemma 1.2. Thus we see that the PFR conjecture would provide s igniﬁcantly more precise information on sets of small doubling constant than Theorem 1.3. We are not able to prove the PFR conjecture in full generality here; however we will be able to verify this conjecture in the special case when A is a downset. To discuss this model setting we let ( ei)i∈I be the standard basis of FI 2, and let ⟨, ⟩: FI 2 × FI 2 → F2 be the standard bilinear form for which ⟨ei, e j⟩ = δ ij. If x ∈ FI 2 then we will write xi := ⟨x, e i⟩ ∈ F2 for the ith coordinate of x, thus x =∑ i∈I xiei. We will write x ⊥ ei if xi = 0. Deﬁnition 1.5 (Shift-minimal downsets) . Let A ⊆ FI 2. We say that A is a downset if, whenever i ∈ I, x ⊥ ei and x + ei ∈ A, we also have x ∈ A. We say that A is shift-minimal if, whenever i < j are elements of I, x ⊥ ei, x ⊥ ej and x + ej ∈ A we have x + ei ∈ A. If A is both a downset and shift-minimal then we say that it is a shift-minimal downset or SMD for short. Remarks. One might usefully think of elements of Fn 2 as n-dimensional vectors with coeﬃcients in {0, 1}. Then A ⊆ Fn 2 is a downset if it is closed under replacing ones by zeros, and it is shift-minimal if it is closed under shifting ones to the left. The terminology comes from the language of set systems, whic h motivated much of our work in this paper. Given a set X ⊆ I we may associate an element xX =∑ j∈X ej of FI 2. This is clearly a bijection: if x ∈ FI 2 we write Xx = {i ∈ I : xi = 1 } for the subset of I associated to x. In this way we see that there is a one-to-one correspondence between sets A ⊆ FI 2 and set systems on I, that is to say collections of subsets of I. We will not make much use of the set system language in this paper , but some readers may ﬁnd it helpful to think in those terms. We can specialise Deﬁnition 1.1 to downsets or SMDs as follow s. Deﬁnition 1.6 (FSMD(K), FD(K), GSMD(K), and GD(K)). Let K ⩾ 1. We deﬁne FSMD(K) to be the least constant such that for any ﬁnite I ⊆ Z+ and any non-empty SMD A ⊆ FI 2 with doubling constant σ [A] ⩽ K, there exists an aﬃne subspace V ⊆ FI 2 of cardinality |V | ⩽ FSMD(K)|A| which contains A. We deﬁne GSMD(K) to be the least constant such that for any ﬁnite I ⊆ Z+ and any non-empty SMD A ⊆ FI 2 with doubling constant σ [A] ⩽ K, there exists a linear subspace V ⊆ FI 2 of cardinality |V | ⩽ |A| such that A is covered by at most GSMD(K) translates of V . We deﬁne FD(K) and GD(K) similarly to FSMD(K) and GSMD(K) but replace “SMD” with “downset” throughout. It is clear that FSMD(K) ⩽ FD(K) ⩽ F (K) and that GSMD(K) ⩽ GD(K) ⩽ G(K). In fact, in §2 we shall use compression arguments to show Lemma 1.7 (Freiman’s theorem is reducible to SMDs) . We have F (K) = FD(K) = FSMD(K) for all K ⩾ 1. 4 BEN GREEN AND TERENCE TAO In particular, in order to prove Theorem 1.3 it suﬃces to work in the SMD model. Unfortunately we are not currently able to obtain a similar r elationship for G(K), GD(K), and GSMD(K). Nevertheless, we have the following result. Theorem 1.8 (PFR for SMDs) . We have K 1. 46601 ≪ GSMD(K) ≪ K 30 for all K ⩾ 1. In fact we have the following slightly stronger statement. Theorem 1.9 (PFR for SMDs, again) . If A ⊆ Fn 2 is a SMD, then A is contained in a union of at most O(σ [A]30) translates of F⌊log2 |A|⌋ 2 . It is clear that the upper bound in Theorem 1.8 follows from Th eorem 1.9 after performing an order-preserving relabeling to reduce to the ambient group FI 2 to Fn 2 for some n. The lower bound comes by considering a Hamming ball whose ra dius is given by the solution to a certain optimization problem: the detai ls of this computation are given in §4. We also have a similar theorem for downsets. Theorem 1.10 (PFR for downsets). We have K 1. 46601 ≪ GD(K) ≪ K 46 for all K ⩾ 1. Again, we have a slightly stronger statement. Theorem 1.11 (PFR for downsets, again) . If A ⊆ Fn 2 is a SMD, then A is contained in a union of at most O(σ [A]46) translates of a coordinate subspace of cardinality at most |A|. We shall prove Theorems 1.9 and 1.11 (and hence Theorems 1.8 a nd 1.10) in §4. It will be clear from the proofs that the exponents 30 and 46 can b e improved, but we do not attempt to optimise them here. The idea of using methods of extremal set theory to look at Fre iman’s theorem orig- inated from our study of the work of Bollob´ as and Leader [2]. Our treatment of the basic theory of compression operators in the next section is closely based on their work. 2. Compressions In this section we set out some notation for the machinery of c ompressions and related structures which we will use throughout the paper, and in par ticular to establish Lemma 1.7 at the end of this section. Throughout this section I is understood to be a ﬁnite set of natural numbers. Deﬁnition 2.1 (Lex order) . Suppose that x =∑ i∈I xiei and that y =∑ i∈I yiei. Then we deﬁne the lexicographical ordering (or lex ordering ) ≺ by deﬁning x ≺ y if and only if, for the largest coordinate j such that xj ̸= yj, we have xj < y j. This is clearly a total ordering. An initial segment in FI 2 is any set of the form A := {x ∈ FI 2 : x ⪯ y} for some y ∈ FI 2. The following lemma is well known. Lemma 2.2 (Adding initial segments of the lex order) . Suppose that A and B are initial segments of the lex order on FI 2. Then so is A + B. Proof. This is easily proved by strong induction on |A| + |B|. In fact if |A| = r and |B| = s and if 0 ⩽ r, s < 2k then the function HS( r, s ) := |A + B| satisﬁes the relations HS(2k + r, s ) = HS( r, s ) + 2k FREIMAN IN FINITE FIELDS 5 and HS(2k + r, 2k + s) = 2 k+1. (The letters HS stand for Hopf and Stiefel, who studied this f unction in a context arising in diﬀerential topology.) Its rˆ ole in the additive combina torics of FI 2 was observed by Yuvinsky [16]. By induction one may conﬁrm (a result of Plagn e [11]) that HS(r, s ) = min j∈N 2j ( ⌈ r 2j ⌉ + ⌈ s 2j ⌉ − 1 ) . We now deﬁne the notion of a compression with respect to a set o f indices. Deﬁnition 2.3 (J-ﬁbres) . Suppose that A ⊆ FI 2 and J ⊆ I. Then we may factor FI 2 = FI\J 2 × FJ 2 in the obvious manner. For each x ∈ FI\J 2 , deﬁne the J-ﬁbre of A at x to be the set Ax := {y ∈ FJ 2 : (x, y ) ∈ A} ⊆ FJ 2 . Deﬁnition 2.4 (J-compressions). Suppose that J ⊆ I and that A ⊆ FI 2. Then we deﬁne the J-compression CJ(A) of A to be the set obtained by replacing each ﬁbre Ax by Ax for all x ∈ FI\J 2 , the initial segment in the lex order on FJ 2 which has the same cardinality as Ax. We say that A is J-compressed if CJ (A) = A. If 0 ⩽ r ⩽ |I|, we say that A is r-compressed if A is J-compressed for all J ⊆ I with |J| ⩽ r. Similarly, we shall refer to a compression operator CJ with |J| ⩽ r as an r-compression. Example. If I := {1, 2, 3} and A := {e1, e 1+e2, e 3}, then C{1}(A) = {0, e 2, e 3}, C{2}(A) = C∅(A) = A, C{1, 2}(A) = {0, e 1, e 3}, and C{1, 2, 3}(A) = {0, e 1, e 2}. The set {0, e 2, e 3} is 1-compressed but not 2-compressed. The following lemma is trivial: Lemma 2.5 (Trivial properties of compressions) . Let J ⊆ I. (i) If A ⊆ FI 2, then |CJ(A)| = |A|, and in particular CJ (A) ⊆ A iﬀ CJ(A) ⊇ A iﬀ CJ(A) = A. (ii) If A ⊆ FI 2, then CJ (CJ (A)) = CJ(A). In other words, CJ (A) is J-compressed. (iii) If A ⊆ B ⊆ FI 2, then CJ (A) ⊆ CJ(B). When the set J has size at most 2, or is equal to I one can describe compressions quite explicitly: Examples (2-compressions and the full compression) . The compression C∅ is trivial: C∅(A) = A for all A. When J = {i} the eﬀect of the compression C{i} is to move x + ei, where x ⊥ ei, to x whenever possible. Suppose that J = {i, j } with i < j and that A is already a downset. Then the eﬀect of the compression C{i,j } is to “shift” x + ej, where x ⊥ ei and x ⊥ ej, to x + ei whenever possible. If J = I, then CI(A) is simply the initial segment of FI 2 with length |A|. From the above description of compressions we immediately o btain Lemma 2.6 (Compressed sets) . Let A ⊆ FI 2. (i) A is always 0-compressed. (ii) A is 1-compressed if and only if it is a downset. (iii) A is 2-compressed if and only if it is an SMD. (iv) A is |I|-compressed if and only if it is an initial segment of FI 2. 6 BEN GREEN AND TERENCE TAO Now, we recall the well-known observations that compressio ns do not increase the size of sumsets. Lemma 2.7 (Compressions and sumsets) . Suppose that A, B ⊆ FI 2 and that J ⊆ I. Then CJ (A) + CJ(B) ⊆ CJ (A + B). In particular |CJ (A) + CJ(B)| ⩽ |A + B|. Proof. We follow Bollob´ as and Leader [2]. We proceed by induction o n I. If J ̸= I then we work ﬁbrewise. For any two ﬁbres Ax, B y ⊆ FJ 2 we have, by the inductive hypothesis, that Ax + By ⊆ Ax + By. Thus for any z ∈ FI\J 2 we have (CJ (A) + CJ (B))z = ⋃ x+y=z (Ax + By) ⊆ ⋃ x+y=z Ax + By = ⋃ x+y=z (Ax + By) = (A + B)z = CJ (A + B)z, the middle step following from the fact that the initial segm ents of the lex order are totally ordered under inclusion. It remains to deal with the case I = J. By applying the inductive hypothesis and Lemma 2.12 we see that it suﬃces to deal with the case in which both A and B are invariant under compressions CJ ′ with J ′ ⊊ I. By an order-preserving relabeling we may take I = {1, . . . , n } for some n. From the compression-invariance assumption we easily conﬁrm that e ach of A and B is either an initial segment of the lex order or else is equal to the specia l set S := Fn−1 2 \ {e1 + · · · + en−1} ∪ {en}, a very slight perturbation of an initial segment of lex. Clea rly we have CI(S) = Fn−1 2 . This easily implies that CI(S) +CI(S) ⊆ CI(S + S), so let us assume that A is an initial segment of lex and that B = S, so that our task is to prove that A + Fn−1 2 ⊆ CI(A + S). If A ⊆ Fn−1 2 then the left-hand side is just Fn−1 2 whilst the right contains CI(S) = Fn−1 2 . If Fn−1 2 ⊆ A then the left-hand side is Fn 2 . A small amount of thought conﬁrms that A + S = Fn 2 also, thereby concluding the proof. As a corollary of Lemma 2.7 and Lemma 2.5(i) we obtain Corollary 2.8 (Compression decreases doubling constants) . Let J ⊆ I and let A ⊆ FI 2 be non-empty. Then σ [CJ (A)] ⩽ σ [A]. Another immediate corollary of Lemma 2.7 and Lemma 2.5(i) is Corollary 2.9 (Compressed sets closed under addition) . Let J ⊆ I. Then the sum of two J-compressed sets is still J-compressed. In particular, from Lemma 2.6 we see that the sum of two downsets is a downset, and the sum of two SMDs is a n SMD. In fact, for downsets one can express sumsets in terms of latt ice operations. FREIMAN IN FINITE FIELDS 7 Deﬁnition 2.10 (Lattice structure) . If x, y ∈ FI 2 then we write x ∨ y := ∑ i∈I max(xi, y i)ei and x ∧ y := ∑ i∈I min(xi, y i)ei. (In the set systems language, these operations correspond t o set union and set intersec- tion respectively.) If If A, B ⊆ FI 2 then we write A ∨ B := {x ∨ y : x ∈ A, y ∈ B}. Lemma 2.11 (Lattice representation of downset addition) . If A, B are downsets then A + B = A ∨ B. Proof. If x ∈ A and y ∈ B, we observe that x + y = x′ ∨ y′, where x′ ∈ A and y′ ∈ B are formed from x, y by zeroing out those coordinates i for which xi = yi = 1. This shows that A + B ⊆ A ∨ B. The identity x ∨ y = x + y′ conﬁrms that A ∨ B ⊆ A + B. The next lemma states that by applying a suitable sequence of compressions to a set A one may obtain a set which is inert with respect to the applica tion of further compressions. Lemma 2.12 (Termination of compression sequences). Let r, 1 ⩽ r ⩽ |I|, be an integer and let A ⊆ FI 2. Then there exists an r-compressed set A′ which can be obtained from A by applying a ﬁnite number of r-compressions. Proof. By the principle of inﬁnite descent, it suﬃces to exhibit a we ight function w : P(FI 2) → Z⩾ 0 with the property that w(CJ(A)) < w (A) whenever CJ is an r- compression and CJ(A) ̸= A. It is then clear that one may ﬁnd a sequence I1, . . . , I k with the required properties and with k ⩽ w(A). There is a wide choice of possible weight functions. One is w(A) := ∑ x∈A 2ρ (x), where ρ (x) is the position of x in the lex order on FI 2. Next, we relate compression to Hamming balls. Deﬁnition 2.13 (Hamming balls) . If x ∈ FI 2 then we deﬁne the Hamming norm of x as ∥x∥ := |{i ∈ I : xi = 1}|. If 0 ⩽ r ⩽ |I| then we deﬁne the Hamming ball Hr(I) := {x ∈ FI 2 : ∥x∥ ⩽ r}. We abbreviate Hr({1, . . . , n }) as Hr(n). Example. We have H1(n) = {0, e 1, . . . , e n}. Proposition 2.14 (Compression and Hamming balls) . Let 0 ⩽ r ⩽ |I|. (i) If 0 ⩽ r ⩽ |I|, then Hr(I) is 2-compressed. (ii) If Hr(I) ⊆ A ⊆ FI 2 and CJ is a 2-compression, then Hr(I) ⊆ CJ (A). (iii) If A ⊆ Hr(I) and CJ is a 2-compression, then CJ (A) ⊆ Hr(I). Proof. One easily checks that Hr(I) is an SMD, and part (i) then follows from Lemma 2.6. Parts (ii) and (iii) then follow from Part (i) and Lemma 2 .5(iii). Remark. Observe that the Hamming balls fail to be 3-compressed in gen eral. Because of this fact, we will only work with 2-compressions for the rest of the paper. We have only included the higher compression operators because (for exa mple) Lemma 2.7 seems 8 BEN GREEN AND TERENCE TAO to be most naturally proven in this more general context, and because these higher compressions may be of use in future applications. Finally, we give some relations between subspaces and the SM D property. Lemma 2.15 (Compressions and subspaces) . Suppose that A ⊆ FI 2 is a SMD and that H ⊆ FI 2 is an aﬃne subspace of dimension d. Let Id be the initial segment of I of length d. (i) For any J ⊆ I, CJ (H) is an aﬃne subspace of dimension d. (ii) H is an SMD if and only if H = FId 2 . (iii) If H ⊆ A then FId 2 ⊆ A. (iv) If A ⊆ H then A ⊆ FId 2 . (v) If A = H then A = FId 2 . Proof. Part (i) can be proven directly. Alternatively, one may use C orollary 2.8 CJ(A) has doubling constant 1 and is therefore an aﬃne subspace, wh ich by cardinality con- siderations must have dimension d. The “if” direction of (ii) is trivial. To prove the “only if” d irection, observe from rank considerations that H must have a full projection onto FJ 2 for at least one set J ⊆ I with |J| = d. In other words, H contains an element with at least d non-zero entries. Using the SMD property this implies that H contains∑ i∈Id ei, and using the downset property we conclude that H contains FId 2 . By cardinality or dimension considerations we conclude H = FId 2 as desired. Parts (iii) and (iv) follow from parts (i), (ii) and Lemma 2.1 2 (as well as Lemma 2.6(iii)). Part (v) follows immediately from parts (iii) an d (iv). We are now in a position to quickly prove Lemma 1.7. Proof of Lemma 1.7. The claim FSMD(K) ⩽ FD(K) ⩽ F (K) is trivial, so it suﬃces to show that F (K) ⩽ FSMD(K). Suppose that A ⊆ FI 2 has doubling at most K; we wish to enclose A in an aﬃne subspace of cardinality at most FSMD(K)|A|. By translation we may make A contain the origin, in which case the aﬃne subspace is a linea r subspace. The claim is then equivalent to the assertion that the linear space spanned by A has dimension at most log 2(FSMD(K)|A|). Suppose that the claim failed. Then we could ﬁnd linearly ind ependent elements v1, . . . , v n in A with n > log2(FSMD(K)|A|), and such that A is contained in the span of v1, . . . , v n. By a linear transformation (and a relabeling of I) we may assume that I ⊇ { 1, . . . , n } and vi = ei for all i = 1 , . . . , n . Note that A is contained in the span of e1, . . . , e n, i.e. in Fn 2 , and so without loss of generality we may take I = {1, . . . , n }. Thus A now contains the Hamming ball H1(n). Applying Lemma 2.12 (and Lemma 2.6(iii)) we may form an SMD A′ by applying a ﬁnite number of 2-compressions to A. By Lemma 2.15(i) we see that A′ still contains H1(n). By Corollary 2.8 we have σ [A′] ⩽ σ [A] ⩽ K. By Deﬁnition 1.1 we thus see that A′ is contained in an aﬃne space H of dimension at most log 2(FSMD(K)|A|). Applying Lemma 2.15(iv) we see that A′ is contained in a space Fm 2 for some m ⩽ log2(FSMD(K)|A|) < n. But this contradicts the fact that A′ contains H1(n). FREIMAN IN FINITE FIELDS 9 3. Sumsets and Hamming balls Throughout this section we ﬁx a ﬁnite set I of natural numbers. We now use com- pression technology to obtain some relationships between s umsets and Hamming balls for arbitrary subsets of FI 2 (not necessarily downsets or SMDs). These results, though somewhat diverse, all conﬁrm that the Hamming ball Hr(n) is a very unstructured set from the additive point of view. We begin with an application of 1-compressions which shows that large subsets of Hamming balls must expand under s umsets. Proposition 3.1 (Expansion in Hamming balls) . Suppose that A, B ⊆ Hr(I) for some r ⩾ 0. Then |A + B| ⩾ 9−r|A||B|. Proof. We are indebted to Oliver Riordan for pointing out the follow ing simple proof; our original proof was signiﬁcantly more complicated. Applying 1-compressions to A and B (using Lemma 2.7 and Lemma 2.12) it suﬃces to consider the case when A and B are 1-compressed, and hence (by Lemma 2.6(ii)) downsets. By Lemma 2.11 it now suﬃces to show that |A ∨ B| ⩾ 9−r|A||B|. To this end, suppose that x ∈ A∨B. Since x ∈ H2r(I), the number of representations of x as a ∨ b is no more than 2r∑ i=0 2i ( 2r i ) = 9 r. The result follows immediately. Next, we use 2-compressions to show that sumsets of large set s must eventually escape Hamming balls. Proposition 3.2 (Expansion out of Hamming balls) . Suppose that A, A ′, A ′′ ⊆ FI 2 is such that |A||A′||A′′| ⩾ ( √ 5 + 2)−r23|I| (3.1) for some r ⩾ 0. Then A + A′ + A′′ ̸⊆H|I|−r(I). In other words, A + A′ + A′′ contains a vector with at least |I| − r ones. Proof. By an order-preserving labeling we may assume I = {1, . . . , n }. Suppose for contradiction that we can ﬁnd A, A ′, A ′′ such that A + A′ + A′′ ⊆ Hn−r(n). (3.2) Applying 2-compressions to A, A ′, A ′′ (using Lemma 2.7, Lemma 2.12, and Lemma 2.14(i)) we see that we can reduce to the case when A, A ′, A ′′ are all SMDs. By Lemma 2.11 (and Corollary 2.9), it now suﬃces to ﬁnd x ∈ A, x ′ ∈ A′, x ′′ ∈ A′′ such that ∥x ∨ x′ ∨ x′′∥ > n − r. We therefore explore conditions under which there are x ∈ A, x ′ ∈ A′, x ′′ ∈ A′′ for which ∥x ∨ x′ ∨ x′′∥ is large. Let us imagine that ∥x∥+ ∥x′∥+ ∥x′′∥ counters have been placed on the points of I, the number of counters placed on i being xi + x′ i + x′′ i . Let us begin at i = n and try to redistribute these counters by moving them to the l eft in such a way that as many elements of I are covered by at least one counter. Thus for i = n, n − 1, n − 2, . . . in turn we pick up all of the counters at that place, and then pu t one counter down if we have one available in our hand. Write S for that set of points which have at least one counter at the end, and set z :=∑ i∈S ei. By construction we see that z = x ∨ x′ ∨ x′′, 10 BEN GREEN AND TERENCE TAO where x is obtained from x by removing some ones from the coordinate vector of x and shifting others to the left, and similarly for x′ and x′′. Since A, A ′, A ′′ are SMDs it follows that x ∈ A, x′ ∈ A′ and x′′ ∈ A′′, and hence that z ∈ A + A′ + A′′. We deﬁne the defect d(x, x ′, x ′′) to be the number of positions which have no counters when our process is ﬁnished, that is to say d(x, x ′, x ′′) := n − |S| = n − ∥z∥. Our aim is to estimate the number of triples ( x, x ′, x ′′) ∈ (Fn 2 )3 for which d(x, x ′, x ′′) ⩾ r. To do this, we ﬁrst imagine a probabilistic variant of the ab ove “moving counters to the left” game which takes place on Z⩽ 0 instead of [ n]. The number of counters on each point 0 , −1, −2, . . . is chosen by tossing three coins, and placing a counter for ea ch coin that comes up heads. All coin tosses are independent. On ce more one imagines moving the counters to the left so as to cover as many points as possible. For n ⩾ 0 we deﬁne the n-defect Dn (which is a random variable) to be the number of positions among {−n, −(n − 1), . . . , −1, 0} which are left uncovered by our process of moving counters to the left. Deﬁne D = sup n⩾ 0 Dn; we will see shortly that this quantity is almost surely ﬁnite. Set q(r) := P(D ⩾ r), thus for instance q(0) = 1. Suppose we play the same game but starting from n = −1 instead of n = 0, ignoring the 0 position entirely. The analogous random variable D′ has the same probability distribution as D, but obeys the relationship D ⩾ r + 1 if and only if D′ ⩾ r + i0 for any ﬁxed r ⩾ 0, where i0 is the number of counters at the position 0. Conditioning this relationship on the random variable i0, we obtain the recurrence relation q(r + 1) = 1 8q(r + 3) + 3 8q(r + 2) + 3 8q(r + 1) + 1 8 q(r) (3.3) for all r ⩾ 0. By conditioning upon the position of the rth uncovered counter after the completion of our process of moving counters to the left, one may also con ﬁrm the multiplicativity property q(r + s) = q(r)q(s) for all r, s ⩾ 1 (note that the claim is trivial if r = 0 or s = 0). Thus q(r) = q(1)r. The characteristic polynomial for the linear recurrence (3.3) , x3 + 3x2 − 5x + 1, factors as (x − 1)(x2 + 4x − 1) and so (since one clearly has q(1) < 1) it follows easily that q(r) = (2 + √ 5)−r. (3.4) Now if x, x ′, x ′′ are chosen independently at random from ( Fn 2 )3 then P(d(x, x ′, x ′′) ⩾ r) ⩽ q(r) = (2 + √ 5)−r. The result follows. Remarks. Our treatment is essentially taken from [4]. In that article upper bounds are obtained for the maximum size of 3-wise t-intersecting set systems: set systems F , F ′, F ′′ such that |F ∩ F ′ ∩ F ′′| ⩾ t for all F ∈ F , F ′ ∈ F ′, F ′′ ∈ F ′′. Equivalently we have |F c ∪ F ′c ∪ F ′′c| ⩽ n − t. This condition is very similar to (3.2), and is in fact equivalent to it in the case that F , F ′ and F ′′ are downsets. An equivalent formulation of Proposition 3.2 is that if we ha ve the largeness condition (3.1), then A + A′ + A′′ + Hr(I) = FI 2. We can now “bootstrap” this proposition to the case in which A, A ′, A ′′ do not have to obey (3.1). FREIMAN IN FINITE FIELDS 11 Proposition 3.3 (Expansion result) . Suppose that A, A ′, A ′′ ⊆ FI 2. Then |A + A′ + A′′ + Hr(I)| ⩾ min ( 1 2 (2 + √ 5)r/ 3|A|1/ 3|A′|1/ 3|A′′|1/ 3, 2|I| ) . Proof. By relabeling we may take I = {1, . . . , n }. Write f (n) := min A,A ′,A ′′⊆Fn 2 |A||A′||A′′|⩽ 8(2+ √ 5)−r23n |A + A′ + A′′ + Hr(n)| |A|1/ 3|A′|1/ 3|A′′|1/ 3 . Claim 3.4. For all n we have f (n) ⩾ 1 2(2 + √ 5)r/ 3. (3.5) Proof. We induct on n. Applying 2-compressions together with Lemma 2.12, Lemma 2.7 and Proposition 2.14(i), we see that the minimum is attai ned when A, A ′, A ′′ are 2-compressed, and thus (by Lemma 2.6(iii)) SMDs. It may be th at the minimum is attained for sets A, A ′, A ′′ with |A||A′||A′′| ⩽ (2 + √ 5)−r23n. Otherwise (of course) the minimum is attained for sets with |A||A′||A′′| ⩾ (2 + √ 5)−r23n. We call these cases 1 and 2 respectively (these cases can overlap if the minimum is achieved multiple times). Suppose that we are in case 1. Split Fn 2 as Fn−1 2 × F2 in some arbitrary direction and write Ai := {x ∈ Fn−1 2 : ( x, i ) ∈ A} for i = 0 , 1. Deﬁne A′ i, A ′′ i similarly. Set p := |A0|/ |A|, p ′ := |A′ 0|/ |A| and p′′ := |A′′ 0|/ |A|. The downset property implies that p, p ′, p ′′ ⩾ 1/ 2; assume without loss of generality that p′′ ⩾ p′ ⩾ p ⩾ 1/ 2. Observe that A + A′ + A′′ + Hr(n) contains disjoint copies of A0 + A′ 0 + A′′ 0 + Hr(n − 1) and A1 + A′ 0 + A′′ 0 + Hr(n − 1). Note also that (p′p′′)1/ 3( p1/ 3 + (1 − p)1/ 3) ⩾ p + p2/ 3(1 − p)1/ 3 ⩾ 1. Since we have |Ai||A′ i′||A′′ i′′| ⩽ 8(2 + √ 5)−r23(n−1) for any choice of i, i ′, i ′′ ∈ {0, 1}, we see that f (n) ⩾ f (n − 1) in this case, and the claim follows by induction hypothesis. In case 2 we are in the situation covered by Proposition 3.2. I t follows from that proposition that A + A′ + A′′ contains a vector with at least n − r ones. It follows that A+A′ +A′′ +Hr(n) contains the vector e1 +· · ·+en and hence, since A+A′ +A′′ +Hr(n) is an SMD, that A + A′ + A′′ + Hr(n) = Fn 2 . Thus f (n) ⩾ 2n |A|1/ 3|A′|1/ 3|A′′|1/ 3 ⩾ 1 2(2 + √ 5)r/ 3, as claimed in (3.5). We return now to the proof of Proposition 3.3. If |A||A′||A′′| ⩽ 8(2 + √ 5)−r23n, then we have |A + A′ + A′′ + Hr(n)| ⩾ 1 2(2 + √ 5)r/ 3|A|1/ 3|A′|1/ 3|A′′|1/ 3. If instead we have |A||A′||A′′| < 8(2+ √ 5)−r23n we may apply Proposition 3.2 to conclude that A + A′ + A′′ + Hr(n) = Fn 2 . In either case we conclude Proposition 3.3. 12 BEN GREEN AND TERENCE TAO 4. The polynomial Freiman-Ruzsa conjecture for SMD sets and downsets We use the above machinery, together with the standard tools of Pl¨ unnecke-Ruzsa theory (see e.g. [15, Chapter 2]) to prove Theorem 1.8, or equ ivalently Theorem 1.9. We ﬁrst prove a slight variant of this theorem, which will be u seful for establishing all of our main theorems. Proposition 4.1 (PFR for SMD sets, intersection version) . Suppose that A ⊆ Fm 2 is a SMD and that σ [A] ⩽ K. Set m := ⌊log2 |A|⌋. Then |A ∩ Fm 2 | ≫ K −29|A|. Proof. Set m′ := m + 5 log2 K + 1. By enlarging n if necessary we may assume that n = m′ + t for some t ⩾ 0. Factor Fm′+t 2 = Fm′ 2 × F{m′+1,...,m ′+t} 2 and consider, for each x ∈ Fm′ 2 , the ﬁbre Ax of A above x. Write B = A ∩ Fm′ 2 for the “base”, and let π : Fm′+t 2 → Fm′ 2 be the projection map. Suppose that A0 contains a vector with Hamming norm r. Since A is a SMD, we conclude that Hr(m′) ⊆ B, and therefore by Proposition 3.3 we have |B + B + B + B| ⩾ |B + B + B + Hr(m′)| ⩾ min ( 1 2(2 + √ 5)r/ 3|B|, 2m′ ) . (4.1) On the other hand, since A is a downset, we have Ax ⊆ A0 for all x ∈ B, and so |A| ⩽ |A0||B|. Also, observe that B + B + B + B + A0 ⊆ A + A + A + A + A, and so |B + B + B + B||A0| ⩽ |A + A + A + A + A|. On the other hand, from the Pl¨ unnecke-Ruzsa inequality (se e [15, Corollary 6.29]) and the hypothesis |A + A| ⩽ K|A| we have |A + A + A + A + A| ⩽ K 5|A| From the preceding three inequalities 2 we conclude |B + B + B + B| ⩽ K 5|B|. The choice of m′ guarantees that K 5|B| ⩽ K 5|A| < 2m′ , and so we are forced to conclude from (4.1) that 1 2(2 + √ 5)r/ 3 ⩽ K 5 and hence that r ⩽ 15 log2+ √ 5 K + 2. In other words, A0 is contained in Hr(F{m+1,...,m +t} 2 ), where r := ⌈15 log2+ √ 5 K + 2⌉. Applying Proposition 3.1 we have |(A + A)x| ⩾ |A0 + Ax| ⩾ 9−r|A0||Ax|, and therefore, summing over x, we see that |A + A| ⩾ 9−r|A0||A|. 2The observant reader will see that this argument can in fact b e easily modiﬁed to the case when A is not a downset, simply by replacing A0 with Ax where x maximises |Ax|. FREIMAN IN FINITE FIELDS 13 It follows immediately that |A0| ⩽ 9rK ≪ K 1+15 log2+ √ 5 9 ⩽ K 24. Now A is a downset, and so Ax ⊆ A0 for every x. It follows immediately that |B| ≫ K −24|A|. Recall that B := A ∩ Fm′ 2 . The fact that A is a downset easily implies that |A ∩ Fj 2|/ 2j is a non-increasing function of j ∈ N. Recalling the deﬁnition of m′ in terms of m it follows that |A ∩ Fm 2 | ≫ K −29|A|, as claimed. This proves Proposition 4.1, and hence the upper bound of Theorem 1.8. Proof of Theorem 1.9. Set K := σ [A], m := ⌊log2 |A|⌋, and B := A ∩ Fm 2 . Applying Ruzsa’s covering lemma (see e.g. [15, Lemma 2.14]) we may cov er A by at most |A+B| |B| translates of B − B. But |A + B| ⩽ |A + A| ⩽ K|A| and B − B ⊆ Fm 2 , while from Proposition 4.1 we have |B| ≫ K −29|A|. The claim follows. Now we turn from SMD sets to downsets. The key proposition is Proposition 4.2 (Small doubling implies escape from Hamming ball) . Let A ⊆ Fn 2 be such that with σ [A] ⩽ K, and such that A + A + A ⊆ Hr(n). Then r ⩾ log2 |A| − 42 log2 K − O(1). Proof. By applying 2-compressions (using Lemma 2.7, Corollary 2.8 , Lemma 2.12 and Proposition 2.14) we may reduce to the case where A is 2-compressed, and hence (by Lemma 2.6(iii)) an SMD set. Let m := ⌊log2 |A|⌋, and set B := A ∩ Fm 2 . Applying Proposition 4.1 we see that |B| ≫ K −29|A| ≫ K −292m. Applying Proposition 3.2 we see that B + B + B contains a vector of norm at least m − 3 log√ 5+2 K 29 − O(1) ⩾ log2 |A| − 42 log2 K − O(1). But B + B + B is contained in A + A + A, which lies in Hr(n) by hypothesis. The claim follows. Proof of Theorem 1.11. Let K := σ [A]. Let A be a downset with σ [A] ⩽ K. By Proposition 4.2 A + A + A contains a vector with Hamming norm at least log 2 |A| − 42 log2 K − O(1). By Corollary 2.9, A + A + A is a downset, and so A + A + A contains a coordinate subspace FJ 2 of cardinality ≫ K −42|A|. Now observe that |A + FJ 2 | ⩽ |A + A + A + A| ⩽ K 4|A| ≪ K 46|FJ 2 | by the Pl¨ unnecke-Ruzsa inequality (see [15, Corollary 6.29]). Thus A can be covered by O(K 46) translates of FJ 2 . If |FJ 2 | ⩽ |A| we are done, so suppose instead that |FJ 2 | > |A|. Then we have |FJ 2 | ⩽ |A + FJ 2 | ⩽ |A + A + A + A| ⩽ K 4|A| ⩽ K 4|FJ 2 | so that A can be covered by O(K 4) translates of FJ 2 , which can in turn be covered by O(K 4) translates of a vector space of cardinality at most |A|. Thus in both cases we obtain Theorem 1.11, and the upper bound of Theorem 1.10 foll ows. 14 BEN GREEN AND TERENCE TAO It remains to establish the lower bound in Theorems 1.8 and 1. 10. Since GSMD(K) ⩽ GD(K) ⩽ G(K), it is clear that the lower bound in Theorem 1.8 is stronger. Proof that GSMD(K) ≫ K 1. 46601. Let α ∈ (0, 1/ 10] be a constant to be optimised later. Let A = H⌊αn ⌋(n) ⊆ Fn 2 ; this is of course a SMD. A standard computation using Stirling’s formula shows that |A| = 2 (h(α )+o(1))n, where h is the entropy function h(α ) := −α log2 α − (1 − α ) log2(1 − α ). Since A + A = H2⌊αn ⌋(n) we have 1 n log2 σ [A] = h(2α ) − h(α ) + o(1). (4.2) Set m := ⌊log2 |A|⌋. Since A is a SMD, we see from Lemma 2.15 that if A is covered by t translates of a subspace of dimension m then it is in fact covered by t translates of Fm 2 . Thus if τ [A] is the minimum possible value of t then we see that τ [A] = |Hαn (n − m)| and hence that 1 n log2 τ [A] = (1 − h(α ))h ( α 1 − h(α ) ) + o(1). (4.3) Using Mathematica we found that the ratio between (4.3) and (4.2) is largest whe n α = 0. 0939288 . . . , in which case it is 1 . 46601 . . . + o(1). Remarks. Proposition 3.2 immediately implies that if A ⊆ Fn 2 is a downset and if |A| = α 2n then A + A + A contains a subspace of dimension at least n − O(log(1/α )). Proposition 4.2 implies a similar conclusion under the assu mption that A is a downset with σ [A] ⩽ K: now A + A + A contains a subspace of dimension at least log 2 |A| − O(log K). Results of a similar type, but with weaker dependencies, a re known without the assumption that A is a downset. If now A is an arbitrary set and |A| = α 2n then A + A + A contains an aﬃne subspace of dimension n − O(α C), whilst if σ [A] ⩽ K then A + A + A contains an aﬃne subspace of dimension log 2 |A| − O(K C). The ﬁrst of these results is essentially due to Bogolyubov [1] (see also [15, Proposition 4.39]), whilst the second follows from the ﬁrst together with Ruzsa’s obser vation [12] that a set with doubling σ [A] ⩽ K is Freiman 6-isomorphic to a subset A′ ⊆ Fm 2 , where |A′| ⩾ K −122m (cf. [15, Lemma 5.26], as well as [7]). It might reasonably be conjectured that the bounds that we ha ve obtained in the downset case hold in general. Thus it is possible that if A ⊆ Fn 2 has size |A| = α 2n then A + A + A contains an aﬃne subspace of dimension at least n − O(log(1/α )). This conjecture, which we call the Polynomial Bogolyubov Conjecture, implies the PFR Conjecture, but seems to be strictly stronger than it. Our re sults verify this conjecture in the downset model. 5. Freiman-type theorems in Fn 2 We now turn our attention to the proof of Theorem 1.3. We ﬁrst n eed a preliminary result: FREIMAN IN FINITE FIELDS 15 Proposition 5.1 (Freiman’s lemma in ﬁnite ﬁelds) . Suppose that A, B ⊆ Fn 2 satisfy H1(n) ⊆ B ⊆ A. Suppose also that |A| ⩽ nC for some C ⩾ 1. Then |A + B| ⩾ ( 1 2n − O(√n log n) ) |B|, where the implied constant depends only on C. Remark. Similar results, with the O(√n log n) replaced by something weaker such as o(n), are available under much weaker hypotheses, for example t hat |A| ⩽ 2n1−δ for some δ > 0. This proposition should be compared with Freiman’s lemma [6], which asserts that if A is a subset of Rd which is not contained in any proper aﬃne subspace then |A + A| ⩾ (d + 1)|A| − 1 2 d(d + 1). Proof. We may assume that n is large, since the claim is vacuously true otherwise. Suppose, as a hypothesis for contradiction, that the result is false. By applying 2- compressions (using Lemma 2.12, Lemma 2.5(iii), Lemma 2.7, and Proposition 2.14) we may assume that A and B are SMDs. The fact that A is a downset, together with the upper bound on |A|, implies that every element of A has Hamming norm at most C log2 n, or in other words A ⊆ HC log2 n(n). Set M := ⌊10C√n log2 n⌋. Claim 5.2. If x ∈ A there cannot be more than one value of i ⩾ M such that xi = 1. Proof. Suppose that there is some x ∈ A such that there are two values of i ⩾ M such that xi = 1. By shifting to the left, we see that H2(M) ⊆ A. We bound from below the number of distinct elements z = a + b with a ∈ H2(M), b ∈ B and a ∧ b = 0. On the one hand the number of choices for the pair ( a, b ) is at least |B| ( M − C log2 n 2 ) ⩾ (8 + o(1))C 2|B|n log2 2 n. On the other hand, no z arises in too many ways as such a sum a + b. Indeed since ∥z∥ ⩽ 2C log2 n, the number of choices for a is no more than (2 C log2 n)2. Comparing these two bounds it follows that |A + B| ⩾ (8 + o(1))C 2(2C log2 n)−2|B|n log2 2 n > (1 − o(1))n|B|. This is contrary to assumption (for n large enough), and so the claim follows. For each i, M ⩽ i ⩽ n, write Bi := {x ∈ B : xi = 1 }. Set B0 := B \⋃ i Bi. Now A + B contains the sets ei + B0 : M ⩽ i ⩽ n and ei + Bj : M ⩽ i, j ⩽ n, i ̸= j. No element lies in more than two of these sets, and therefore |A + B| ⩾ 1 2 ( (n − M)|B0| + (n − M − 1) n∑ j=M |Bj| ) ⩾ n − M − 1 2 |B|. This concludes the proof of Proposition 5.1. Proof of Theorem 1.3. In view of Lemma 1.2 and Lemma 1.7, it suﬃces to show that FSMD(K) ⩽ 22K+O( √ K log K). 16 BEN GREEN AND TERENCE TAO Let A be an SMD, and let n be the largest integer such that H1(n) ⊆ A. Since A is a downset, we see that A ⊆ Fn 2 . It will thus suﬃce to show that n ⩽ log2 |A| + 2K + O( √ K log K). Let m := ⌊log2 |A|⌋ and factor Fn 2 = Fm 2 × Fn′ 2 where n′ := n − m. For each x ∈ Fm 2 write Ax for the ﬁbre Ax := {y ∈ Fn′ 2 : (x, y ) ∈ A}. Let B be the “base”: B := A ∩ Fm 2 = {x : Ax ̸= ∅}. Since A is a downset, we have Ax ⊆ A0 for all x. Now A + A contains B + A0, and therefore |B||A0| = |B + A0| ⩽ K|A|. On the other hand from Proposition 4.1 we have |B| ≫ K −29|A|. Comparing these two bounds we see that |A0| ≪ K 30. If n′ ⩽ K then we are done, and if not the hypotheses of Lemma 5.1 are satisﬁed and so |Ax + A0| ⩾ ( n′ 2 − O( √ n′ log n′) ) |Ax| for all x. It follows immediately that K|A| ⩾ |A + A| ⩾ ∑ x |Ax + A0| ⩾ ( n′ 2 − O( √ n′ log n′) ) ∑ x |Ax| ⩾ ( n′ 2 − O( √ n′ log n′) ) |A|, and therefore n′ ⩽ 2K + O( √ K log K). This concludes the proof. 6. The small K case Most of our paper has been concerned with the behaviour of qua ntities such as F (K) and G(K) in the asymptotic limit K → ∞ . For sake of completeness we make some remarks about the case of small K, which involves rather diﬀerent techniques to those employed above. In [3] it was shown that F (K) ⩽ 2K−1 3K−K 2−1 for 1 ⩽ K ⩽ 12 5 , together with a more complicated upper bound for F (K) in the region 12 5 < K < 4. Here we give a precise formula for F (K) in the region 1 ⩽ K < 9 5. Proposition 6.1 (F (K) for small K). If K < 7 4, then F (K) = K, while F (K) = 8 7K for 7 4 ⩽ K < 9 5. (In particular, F is discontinuous. ) FREIMAN IN FINITE FIELDS 17 Proof. The lower bound F (K) ⩾ K (for any K) is clear by setting A to be a random subset of a large aﬃne space V of density arbitrarily close to 1 /K . The lower bound F (K) ⩾ 8 7 K for K ⩾ 7 4 follows by setting A to be a random subset of {0, e 1, e 2, e 3}×Fn−3 2 with n large of density arbitrarily close to 7 4K , where e1, . . . , e n is the standard basis of Fn 2 . Now we show F (K) ⩽ K for K < 7 4, using an argument from [15, Corollary 5.6]. From Kneser’s theorem (see e.g. [15, Theorem 5.5]) we have |A + A| ⩾ 2|A| − | H|, where H is the largest subspace of Fn 2 with the property that A + A is a union of cosets of H. Since |A + A| < 7 4|A|, we conclude that |A + A| < 7|H|, thus A + A is the union of at most six cosets of H. In particular, if B := ( A + H)/H ⊆ Fn 2 /H , then B + B has cardinality at most 6, and B + B cannot be expressed as the union of cosets of any non-trivial subspace of Fn 2 /H . Kneser’s theorem again gives |B + B| ≥ 2|B| − 1 and thus |B| ⩽ 3. If |B| = 2 or |B| = 3 then B + B is a subspace of dimension 1 or 2 respectively, which is not permitted, so B is a singleton. Thus A is contained in a coset V of H. Since |V | = |A + A| ⩽ K|A|, we obtain F (K) ⩽ K as desired. Now take 7 4 ⩽ K < 9 5. Repeating the above argments with the same notation we obtain that |B + B| < 9 and hence |B| ⩽ 4. The only new case is when |B| = 4, in which case B + B is contained in a subspace of dimension 3 and has cardinality exactly 7. This implies that A is contained in an aﬃne subspace of cardinality 8 |H|. Since 7|H| = |A + A| ⩽ K|A|, the bound F (K) ⩽ 8 7K follows. Remark. A similar analysis can (in principle) be used to compute F (K) exactly for any given K < 2, though the number of cases to consider via this method grow s quickly to inﬁnity as K → 2. The same analysis also gives precise values for G(K) when G is small. Proposition 6.2 (G(K) for small K). If 1 < K < 7 4, then GSMD(K) = GD(K) = G(K) = 2 , while GSMD(K) = GD(K) = G(K) = 3 for 7 4 ⩽ K < 9 5. Proof. The lower bound GSMD(K) ⩾ 2 for 1 < K < 7 4 can be established by setting A to be an initial segment in a large space Fn 2 of density arbitrarily close to 1 /K . The lower bound GSMD(K) ⩾ 3 for 7 4 ⩽ K < 9 5 can be established by setting A to be the Cartesian product (in Fn 2 × F3 2 = Fn+3 2 ) of an initial segment in a large space Fn 2 of density arbitrarily close to 7 4K , together with the set {0, e n+1, e n+2, e n+3}. Since GSMD(K) ⩽ GD(K) ⩽ G(K), it thus suﬃces to establish the bounds G(K) ⩽ 2 for K < 7 4 and G(K) ⩽ 3 for K < 9 5. If K < 7 4, then by the analysis used to prove Lemma 6.1 we see that A is contained in an aﬃne space V of size |V | ⩽ K|A|. Subdividing V into two aﬃne spaces of size at most K|A|/ 2 ⩽ |A| we obtain the bound G(K) ⩽ 2. (The case when |V | = 1 can of course be dealt with by hand.) If K < 9 5, the only new case is when A is the union of four cosets of H, where 7|H| ⩽ K|A|. In particular, we see that 2 |H| ⩽ 2 7 9 5|A| < |A|. Covering these four cosets of H by three cosets of a subspace of one higher dimension than H we obtain the bound G(K) ⩽ 3. Remark. While these above small examples show that GSMD(K) = GD(K) = G(K) for small K, we do not expect this relationship to hold exactly in genera l; it may be that there are non-downsets of reasonably small doubling consta nt which are signiﬁcantly 18 BEN GREEN AND TERENCE TAO harder to cover eﬃciently by subspaces than downsets or SMDs with the same doubling constant. Certainly the arguments used to prove Lemma 1.7 do not seem to extend to the G() family of quantities. On the other hand, Conjecture 1.4 wo uld clearly imply that GSMD(K), GD(K), and G(K) are equivalent up to polynomial factors. References [1] N. N. Bogolyubov, Sur quelques propri´ et´ es arithm´ etiques des presque-p´ eriodes, Ann. Chaire Math. Phys. Kiev 4 (1939), 185–194. [2] B. Bollob´ as and I. Leader, Sums in the grid, Discrete Math. 162 (1996), no. 1-3, 31–48. [3] J. M. Deshouillers, F. Hennecart, A. Plagne, On small sumsets in (Z/ 2Z)n, Combinatorica 24 (1) (2004), 53–68. [4] P. Frankl, The shifting technique in extremal set theory, in Surveys in combinatorics 1987 (New Cross, 1987), 81–110, London Math. Soc. Lecture Note Ser., 123, Cambridge Univ. Press, Cam- bridge, 1987. [5] G. Freiman, Foundations of a structural theory of set add ition. Translated from the Russian. Translations of Mathematical Monographs 37, American Mathematical Society, Providence, R. I., 1973. vii+108 pp. [6] G. Freiman, Structure theory of set addition , Asterisque 258 (1999), 1–33. [7] B. J. Green, Finite ﬁeld models in additive combinatorics, in Surveys in Combinatorics 2005, London Math. Soc. Lecture Notes 327, 1–27. [8] B. J. Green and I. Z. Ruzsa, Sets with small sumset and rectiﬁcation, Bull. London Math. Soc. 38 (2006), no. 1, 43-52. [9] B. J. Green and I. Z. Ruzsa, Freiman’s theorem in an arbitrary abelian group , to appear in J. London Math. Soc. [10] B. J. Green and T. C. Tao, A note on the Freiman and Balog-Szemer´ edi-Gowers theorems in ﬁnite ﬁelds, submitted. Available at: http://www.arxiv.org/abs/math.CO/0701585. [11] A. Plagne, Additive number theory sheds extra light on the Hopf-Stiefe l ◦ function, (English sum- mary) Enseign. Math. (2) 49 (2003), no. 1-2, 109–116. [12] I. Ruzsa, Generalized arithmetical progressions and sumsets , Acta Math. Hungar. 65 (1994), no. 4, 379–388. [13] I. Z. Ruzsa, An analog of Freiman’s theorem in groups, Structure theory of set addition, Ast´ erisque 258 (1999), 323–326. [14] T. Sanders, A note on Freiman’s theorem in vector spaces , preprint. Available at http://www.arxiv.org/abs/math.NT/0605523. [15] T. C. Tao and V. H. Vu, Additive Combinatorics, CUP 2006. [16] S. Yuzvinsky, Orthogonal pairings of Euclidean spaces, Michigan Math. J. 28 (1981), no. 2, 131– 145. Centre for Mathematical Sciences, Wilberforce Road, Cambridg e CB3 0W A, Eng- land. E-mail address : b.j.green@dpmms.cam.ac.uk Department of Mathematics, UCLA. source_title: Freiman's theorem in finite fields via extremal set theory
approachable_angle: Candidate approach signalled by 'bound' in the statement — worth a computational search/bound-tightening pass.

statement: [novelty_signal: unclassified] Really I should first ask this question here on MathOverflow and only then post it as an open problem in Open Problem Garden and propose it as a polymath problem .
source_title: Do filters complementive to a given filter form a complete lattice?

statement: Indeed I did the reverse and now hope that for somebody my "open problem" may be simple.
source_title: Do filters complementive to a given filter form a complete lattice?

statement: [novelty_signal: unclassified] (Note to those who like to tidy LaTeX, or ${\rm \LaTeX}$: I kindly request that you don't put any LaTeX in the title of this question, nor change the bolds below to blackboard bold.)$\newcommand{\FA}{{\rm A}}$ The question in the title might be an open problem, but the people I've consulted aren't sure.
source_title: Does anybody know if the Fourier algebra of SL(3,Z) has an approximate identity?

statement: [novelty_signal: unclassified] One open question in extremal graph Theory is the so-called Zarankiewicz problem (see for instance the wikipedia page), which ask for the maximum number of edges in a bipartite graph with a fixed number of vertices without $K_{l,r}$ as a subgraph.
However, since Valiant’s original paper, it remains an open problem to ﬁnd a n explicit ‘Valiant-rigid’ matrix.
source_title: Matrix rigidity and the Croot-Lev-Pach lemma

statement: It is an inte resting open problem to determine if Theorem 1.3 still holds if one is allowed to take a function f : Fn q ↦→F where F is the rational numbers (or even the complex numbers).
source_title: Matrix rigidity and the Croot-Lev-Pach lemma

statement: It was an open question whether r(n) ≤ cn for some c < q .
source_title: Matrix rigidity and the Croot-Lev-Pach lemma

statement: In this paper, we give a characterizations of $\sigma$-dispersive group, which give a positive answer to an open problem of Skiba in the paper.
source_title: On an open problem of Skiba
stated_by: Zhen-Lin Wu, Chi Zhang, W. Guo

statement: Let A be a set of squares. Is it possible to prove that the equation x − y = z, x, y, z ∈ A − A has |A − A|2−c solutions? Here c > 0 is an absolute constant. 9 Acknowledgements The research of the second author was supported in part by an N SERC Discovery grant and OTKA NK grant. The work of the second author was also supporte d by the European Research Council (ERC) under the European Union’s Horizon 2020 resea rch and innovation programme (grant agreement No. 741420, 617747, 648017). source_title: The Uniformity Conjecture in Additive Combinatorics

statement: Our result not only extends a previous theorem of Frankl, Liu, Wang and Yang on separated families, but also provides explicit counterexamples to an open problem proposed by them, thereby settling their problem in the negative.
source_title: Two results on set families: Sturdiness and intersection
stated_by: Yongjiang Wu, Zhiyi Liu, Lihua Feng, Yongtao Li
